This research advances IT governance practices by providing organizations with a valuable tool to automate and improve IT strategies, effectively achieving business objectives. user_input: I. INTRODUCTION I. INTRODUCTION The use of drones has become increasingly common in various applications, including aerial photography, surveying, and monitoring. However, drones can also be involved in incidents such as accidents, violations, or even crimes, making it necessary to investigate drone logs as part of forensic investigations. In a forensic investigation, drone logs are essential sources of information that can help investigators reconstruct the events leading up to an incident. Drone logs typically include information such as flight paths, altitude, speed, battery life, sensor data, and other telemetry information . The ﬁrst ﬁve phases were used to locate and validate the drone’s sensors and data. However, the proposed framework has not explained the preparation phase in detail. Therefore, another framework consisting of four investigative phases with a more comprehensive analysis is proposed by Al-Dhaqm et al. . reference: I. INTRODUCTION I. INTRODUCTION The use of drones has become increasingly common in various applications, including aerial photography, surveying, and monitoring. However, drones can also be involved in incidents such as accidents, violations, or even crimes, making it necessary to investigate drone logs as part of forensic investigations. In a forensic investigation, drone logs are essential sources of information that can help investigators reconstruct the events leading up to an incident. Drone logs typically include information such as flight paths, altitude, speed, battery life, sensor data, and other telemetry information . Important analysis of drone logs involves the identification of the most critical pieces of information in the log data and the analysis of how they relate to the incident

In a forensic setting, analysing a large number of log entries resulting from binary anomaly detection is considered inefficient and labour-intensive as the detection is performed on the acquired evidence at one time . Therefore, too many detected anomalies need to be detailed into a more fine- grained detection to help the investigator better prioritise the analysis objective. As a result, this study extends the binary anomaly detection to multi-class anomaly detection, as exemplified by another domain application such as plane flight data , image data , and internet of vehicle . Point anomaly detection characterises each log entry, whether an event is an abnormality or a normal event. For instance, if a user types the wrong password when trying to gain access to an operating system prompt, the generated log message can be considered as an anomaly , , . As for contextual anomaly, the detection involves analysing some contextually related attributes to determine whether a log entry is an anomaly. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK In this paper, we demonstrate how to train a log-based anomaly detection model that can prioritise the prediction of higher severity anomalies on drone flight logs while increasing the prediction confidence score at the same time. Considering the nature of the dataset, where samples that belong to different severity levels share common features, a multitask vector label representation along with severity-oriented decoding is proposed. An extensive experiment proved that the proposed approach is better than the previous work baselines while being inferior to our baseline scenario based on the accuracy and F1

Instead of relying on resource-intensive transfer learning models, our approach optimizes model efficiency, making it more com- putationally friendly or resource-constrained applications. This contribution aims to streamline the implementation of emotion recognition systems without compromising perfor- mance. The proposed research is based on a simplified deep learn- ing architecture and channel selection approach that leads towards closer and resource-constrained real-time implemen- tation. In addition, research for cross-subject validation is more likely to be implemented widely than dependent subject validation. Cross-subject validation has a better generaliza- tion model than dependent-subject validation. reference: DATASET The DEAP dataset has 32 participants with a composition of 50% male and 50% female samples . The stimulus used is a video with a duration of 60 seconds. The results of the EEG recording have a duration of 63 seconds, of which 3 seconds are the baseline. One participant has 40 recordings of the EEG trials. The results of the EEG trial recordings have been mea- sured using the Self-Assessment Manikin (SAM). SAM has four emotional dimensions, namely valence, arousal, domi- nance, and liking, with ratings 0-9. For valence and arousal classes, emotion labels are divided into two categories: low and high. reference: Recall evaluates the model's ability to capture all the objects that should have been identified, emphasizing the importance of minimizing false negatives, which is crucial in many real-world applications. Recall was chosen because the goal was to reduce the amount of false negatives. As this system was aimed at the automatic flagging process of defective 3D printing results, it is imperative to reduce the amount of missed defects. CONCLUSION This research demonstrates that the YOLOv8 model, specifically the YOLOv8m variant, provides excellent results in detecting Peritoneal Carcinomatosis (PC) from the used laparoscopic image dataset. By utilizing and fine-tuning the pre-trained YOLOv8 weights, this model shows superior performance with a mean Average Precision (mAP50) of 0.871, precision of 0.893, recall of 0.785, and an F1 score of 0.84. The predicted images also show that the performance of YOLOv8 with pre-trained weights is able to predict images well. All YOLOv8 scales are accurate in making predictions, with no errors in image prediction. In contrast, without using pre-trained weights, YOLOv8n and YOLOv8s still make errors in prediction, with some lesions being missed.

Models pretrained on these datasets have optimized weights for recognizing common features, mitigating issues associated with small datasets. Transfer learning and fine-tuning allow models to adapt to new datasets, significantly reducing training time. Studies utilizing pretrained models include for detect- ing polyps with YOLOv4, for Crohn’s disease lesions, for pre-colorectal cancer abnormalities, for bladder cancer lesions, and for cervical lesions.
